content.full_text:
FPPC
JAN 16 9:48 AM '91

OFFICE OF THE

CITY ATTORNEY

January 14, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

-and-

Jill R. Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division
428 "J" Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento CA. 95804

Re:    Request for reconsideration of
       Written Advice (Govt. Code Section 83114(b))
       Your file No. A-90-499

Dear Mr. Hallabrin and Ms. Stecher:

As the Acting City Attorney for the City of South San Francisco (effective
January 2, 1991) it is respectfully requested on behalf of Councilmember John
Penna that there be a reconsideration of the written advice letter dated
October 12, 1990, based upon the Request for Written Advice letter dated July
27, 1990, of the former City Attorney (a copy of each letter is attached for
easy reference).

It is acknowledged by Councilmember Penna that he read and made no objection
to the content of the July 27, 1990 letter before it was mailed to the FPPC.
However, after receipt and review of the Advice Letter dated October 12, 1990,
with reflection upon how certain phrases were used together with incomplete
statements in the July 27, 1990 Request Letter, Councilmember Penna is of the
belief that a clarification of facts may result in a different conclusion so
that he may participate in decisions for which he was elected.

Councilmember Penna believes he has no present or future conflict of interest
regarding decisions on the Shearwater project, but he has and will faithfully
abide by the advice of the FPPC.

The essential point is that Councilmember Penna does not intend to pursue
business opportunities relating to the Shearwater project, which is one of
three separate redevelopment project areas in the City.

                           315 Maple Avenue 94080
              City Hall Annex          P.O. Box 711 94083          (415) 877-8515

Fair Political Practice Commission
Page 2
January 14, 1991

MODIFIED BACKGROUND

The City of South San Francisco is a community of approximately 52,000
persons. There are 1,500 acres in the City limits zoned for commercial and
industrial use, out of a total of 9.53 square miles. Approximately one fourth
of the City is zoned commercial and industrial and three-fourths is zoned
residential.

Prior to 1985, the City Council, acting as the City Redevelopment Agency,
formed two presently active redevelopment project areas, namely the Downtown
project area, and the Gateway project area.

In 1986, at the behest of a developer, the City Council and Redevelopment
Agency (same members as City Council) created a third redevelopment project
known as Shearwater for a site encompassing 54 acres of dry land and 133 acres
of water. A master planned mixed use development is proposed.

The Shearwater project area is under one ownership together with six acres
(called the "GSA" parcel) owned by the City. A tentative map for 12 lots on
the non-city owned property was approved in 1986, but a lawsuit filed in 1986
by the City of San Francisco challenged the tentative map and the EIR. In
March 1989, the Court of Appeal remanded to the trial court to reconsider the
residential portion of the Plan by reason of CEQA factors and proximity to the
San Francisco Airport. The litigation delayed progress on the project. A
supplemental EIR was prepared in 1990.

The City owned GSA parcel is considered to be integral to the development.
Pursuant to an outstanding agreement with the developer, the City anticipates
selling or leasing the GSA parcel to the developer. Details of the
transaction are being negotiated by the City Manager and the developer,
subject to direction and ultimate approval by the City Council. The
transaction was expected to conclude by fall of 1990. The developer has had
financial problems with backers from Japan and due to the litigation delay.
As of this date there is no assurances of concluding any transaction.

The individual components of the project, such as the approval of the final
subdivision map and all of the approvals for the buildings (retail, office,
hotel, possible condominiums) as well as the specific design of a marina, will
be subject to various discretionary approval processes as the project is
constructed in phases. In addition, the developer has requested the City to
establish an assessment district and a landscape and lighting maintenance
district for various financing purposes. The tentative map expiration date is
December 31, 1991, which reflects a stay for the litigation period.

In November 1989, John Penna was elected to the City Council. Mr. Penna is a
real estate broker and has owned Penna Realty, a local real estate company,
for the past 26 years. He operates as a one-man office without any agents.
His only office is in the Downtown redevelopment area. Over 60% of

Fair Political Practice Commission
Page 3
January 14, 1991

Mr. Penna's business is done in South San Francisco. The business includes
management of properties within the Downtown redevelopment area, real estate
sales of residential, commercial and industrial property, and real estate
appraising and consulting.

In December 1989 and January 1990, the Shearwater project began to move
forward again after the litigation hiatus. Councilmember Penna participated
in some discussions and fairly routine development actions. He received
briefings on ownership structure and an appraisal report pertaining to the
City's GSA parcel.

TRI Realtors is the agent for marketing and leasing the Shearwater site. Any
broker, however, can bring a client directly to the developer and receive a
full commission for the leasing or sale of space in the project.

During March 1990, Councilmember Penna approached the Shearwater developer,
whom he has known since 1984, to advise the developer that he was going to the
upcoming International Association of Realtors (FIABCI) conference in
Acapulco, Mexico. He offered to present the Shearwater Project hotel site
offering at the FIABCI conference at his own expense. Pursuant to an
authorizing letter, obtained by Councilmember Penna from Shearwater at the
request of FIABCI, he made a presentation on the Shearwater Project hotel site
to approximately 1,000 brokers attending the conference, using materials
supplied by the developer. If a broker came forward as a result of that
presentation and went through Councilmember Penna to present an offer,
Councilmember Penna could have received a commission. The authority expired
at the conclusion of the FIABCI conference in May 1990. To date, no broker
has come forward and none are contemplated.

In April, 1990, Councilmember Penna advised the former City Attorney of his
authority and intention to make a presentation on behalf of Shearwater at the
FIABCI conference on the hotel site. In the course of his disclosure he
explained how he conducted his business. The former City Attorney assumed
from his explanation and incorrectly presented in the July 27, 1990 Request
for Advice Letter that Councilmember Penna had been working with a broker
prior to the conference on the Shearwater hotel site. Councilmember Penna's
sole activity has been the presentation of the hotel site at the FIABCI
conference in May 1990, and his authority was then concluded. Councilmember
Penna did not receive any monetary compensation and has had no financial gain.

In May the former City Attorney advised Councilmember Penna that due to the
then current negotiations for the hotel and the pending presentation in Mexico
that he should refrain from participating in several Shearwater matters coming
before the Agency/Council, and he followed that advice. Since the approvals
being sought relate to the financial feasibility of the project and since the
discussion concerning the terms of sale of the City-owner property have a
financial impact on the project, to the extent Councilmember Penna was
pursuing a business relationship with the developer it was the opinion of the
former City Attorney, after seeking oral advice from the FPPC, that it did not

Fair Political Practice Commission
Page 4
January 14, 1991

seem appropriate for Councilmember Penna to participate in Agency/Council
business.

As indicated previously, to date there have not been any leads, listings,
pending or completed sales resulting in a financial return to Councilmember
Penna. Consequently, he believes he has a right to participate in future
Agency/Council discussions and votes concerning various aspects of the
project, including the lease or sale of public land and the
formation/implementation of assessment and maintenance districts. He also
believes that as a realtor he has a right to participate in transactions
throughout the City when he has no conflict of interest. Councilmember Penna
acknowledges that as an active real estate broker a business opportunity could
occur in the Shearwater project just as within any other area or neighborhood
of the City, but that he has no present or future intention of pursuing
business opportunities in the Shearwater project. In short, the FIABCI
presentation was a one-shot effort and it has been concluded without success.

PERSONAL ANALYSIS

Councilmember Penna contends that in view of his office location, property
ownership, and property management business all being in the Downtown
redevelopment area and having no property or business interests in the
Shearwater project, the decisions of Downey Cares v. Downey Community
Development Com. 196 Cal.App.3d 983 (1987) and Witt v. Morrow 70 Cal.App.3d
817 (1977), and the FPPC opinion of In re Oglesby, are not in point since each
involve an individual with property ownership and interests within the subject
redevelopment project areas. Councilmember Penna holds no property or
business interests in the Shearwater project area.

The Shearwater project area is separate from the two other redevelopment
project areas resulting in an action for one having no effect on the other
project areas.

Councilmember Penna acknowledges that as an active real estate broker his door
is open to receive business and that he invites business generally through
advertising, but he questions the use of the phrase "that he may pursue
opportunities related to the Shearwater project,..." ("Question" on page 1,
and page 6, line 7 of advice letter dated October 12, 1990). To "pursue"
denotes "to find or employ measures to obtain or accomplish." Rather than
"pursue" perhaps a better term is that as a real estate broker Councilmember
Penna is "accessible", "capable of being reached", for business opportunities
generally.

Finally, Councilmember Penna contends that if there is a foreseeability under
facts presented above that a decision by him on the Shearwater project will
result in a material financial effect or gain on his business, then such is
tantamount to holding that every active real estate broker or agent elected to
the council has a foreseeable conflict of interest on all land use matters
since there is a possibility that any given council or agency action may

Political Practlve Commission
January 14, 1991
Page 5

enhance property values or result in greater numbers of property transactions
anywhere in the City, and thus produce financial gain to council elected
realtors by being accessible to possible, but not necessarily, prospective
opportunities.

QUESTIONS

1. If Councilmember Penna participates now as a member of the
Agency/Council in discussions and actions concerning the Shearwater project,
and six months, or eighteen months, from now as the project is underway he has
a client who wishes to engage in lease or purchase of a portion of the
project, is there a conflict and when does it arise?

2. If, as a member of the Agency/Council, Councilmember Penna
participates in decisions that enable the project to go forward, does that
preclude him from being involved in the project in his capacity as a realtor?

3. When a project, such as Shearwater, has numerous integrated parts and
will require multiple approvals for different phases over a period of time, is
there a conflict of interest if a councilmember actively seeks to transact
private business during the pendency of a phase of the project or the entire
project?

4. If a City Council is engaging in negotiation and sale or lease
transactions with a developer, is there a conflict of interest if a
councilmember participates in those activities and subsequently engages in
private business transactions, particularly if the councilmember has no
specific deal pending at the time of the negotiation and sale or lease?

5. As an additional, but more specific question, may Councilmember Penna
be present at either open or closed sessions of the Agency/Council when
information regarding Shearwater or the GSA Parcel sites are discussed?

Respectfully submitted,

DONALD R. HAILE
Acting City Attorney

DRH/mm

cc: Blanche Breeze, FPPC Counsel
Councilmember Penna
City Manager Jesús Armas

sections.question_synthetic: Councilmember Penna seeks reconsideration of a prior FPPC advice letter regarding whether his participation in Shearwater project decisions creates a conflict of interest given his role as a real estate broker who offered the hotel site at a conference and could potentially receive commissions from future transactions related to the project.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document is an incoming request letter seeking reconsideration of prior advice, not an FPPC response letter. The questions section appears to be truncated at the end of the provided document. This is correspondence FROM the City Attorney TO the FPPC requesting reconsideration, not a response FROM the FPPC.

embedding.summary: This is a request for reconsideration of FPPC written advice (File No. A-90-499) dated October 12, 1990, submitted by the Acting City Attorney on behalf of Councilmember John Penna of South San Francisco regarding potential conflicts of interest related to his participation in decisions concerning the Shearwater redevelopment project while serving as an active real estate broker.
